title: The Conqueror
subtitle: Chandragupta seizes the throne, defeats the Greeks, and builds India's first great empire
bullets: Chandragupta Maurya overthrew the last Nanda king around 322 BCE with guidance from his mentor Chanakya, a Brahmin scholar who had vowed to destroy the Nanda dynasty after being humiliated at their court.

He first swept through the Greek garrisons Alexander had left in the Punjab, then turned east to seize Magadha and its capital Pataliputra, described by the Greek ambassador Megasthenes as 9 miles long with 570 towers and 64 gates.

Chandragupta defeated the Seleucid king Seleucus Nicator around 305 BCE, gaining Afghanistan, Balochistan, and eastern Iran in exchange for 500 war elephants that later decided a major Hellenistic battle at Ipsus.

By the end of his reign the Maurya Empire stretched roughly 2,000 miles from the Hindu Kush to the Bay of Bengal — one of the largest empires on earth at a time when China was still a century away from unification.

The Uttarapatha highway ran roughly 1,200 miles from Pataliputra to Taxila with milestones, shade trees, and rest houses — modeled on the Persian Royal Road and still the spine of Indian road networks today.

Standardized punch-marked silver coins called karshapana created a uniform monetary system across the subcontinent for the first time, part of a global pattern of states independently discovering coinage.
Chandragupta reportedly abdicated the throne in old age, became a Jain monk, and starved himself to death in the Jain practice of santhara — the first Maurya emperor ending as a religious ascetic.

The partnership between Chanakya the pragmatic philosopher and Chandragupta the ambitious prince is one of history's great pairings — think Aristotle and Alexander, except Chanakya was far more hands-on.

title: The Arthashastra
subtitle: Kautilya's ruthless treatise on statecraft — India's answer to Machiavelli, written 1,800 years earlier
bullets: The Arthashastra is attributed to Chanakya but was probably compiled and revised over several centuries — the version we have may date from the early centuries CE rather than 300 BCE, though the core ideas are likely Maurya-era.

The manuscript was lost for centuries and rediscovered in 1905 by the scholar R. Shamasastry in a palm-leaf manuscript at a library in Mysore — one of the great textual rediscoveries of the 20th century.

The treatise covers everything from spy networks and assassination techniques to taxation, irrigation, mining, trade regulation, animal husbandry, and the proper management of prostitutes and gambling houses.

The Arthashastra's spy system classified agents into categories including wandering monks, merchants, students, fortune-tellers, and poisoners — intelligence gathering was not supplementary to governance, it was governance.

Chanakya's political philosophy has been compared to both Machiavelli's Prince and Han Feizi's Legalism — all three arrived independently at the conclusion that effective government requires understanding power as it actually works.
The bureaucratic system described in the Arthashastra divided the empire into provinces, districts, and villages, with appointed officials, standardized tax rates, and regular inspections.

Sudarshana Lake in Gujarat, an artificial reservoir attributed to the Maurya period, is one of the earliest documented irrigation projects in India — water management was central to the Arthashastra's vision of state power.

title: The Heir
subtitle: Bindusara expands south, patronizes the Ajivikas, and produces a succession crisis
bullets: Bindusara expanded the empire across the Deccan plateau to within 300 miles of India's southern tip, conquering a dry, rocky, mountainous landscape vastly different from the northern river valleys.
He maintained Greek diplomatic contacts — the Seleucid ambassador Deimachus visited Pataliputra, and Bindusara reportedly asked the Seleucid king for sweet wine, dried figs, and a philosopher.

Bindusara patronized the Ajivikas, a now-extinct determinist sect that believed every event was predestined by cosmic fate — the Buddha considered their founder Makkhali Gosala his most dangerous intellectual opponent.
The Maurya dynasty showed remarkable religious flexibility: Chandragupta ended as a Jain, Bindusara patronized the Ajivikas, and Ashoka would become Buddhism's greatest champion.

Ashoka served as provincial governor at Ujjain, the capital of the western province of Avanti, gaining administrative experience and the loyalty of the western army far from the court's succession politics.

When Bindusara died around 272 BCE a violent succession crisis erupted — Buddhist tradition claims Ashoka killed ninety-nine brothers, which is literary hyperbole, but the accession was clearly bloody and contested.

title: Before the Conversion
subtitle: the Kalinga War — 100,000 dead, and the carnage that changed everything
bullets: For roughly the first decade of his reign Ashoka governed as a conventional Indian emperor — expanding territory, maintaining the spy network, running the Arthashastra's administrative machinery.

The Buddhist literary tradition paints pre-conversion Ashoka as a monster called Chandashoka (Ashoka the Fierce), with elaborate cruelties designed to heighten the dramatic contrast with his later transformation.
Around 260 BCE Ashoka invaded Kalinga on the eastern coast, the last major independent territory in the subcontinent — the war killed or displaced hundreds of thousands.

Rock Edict XIII, Ashoka's own account, states that 100,000 were killed, 150,000 deported, and many times that number perished — numbers that are stylized but indicate carnage on a scale that genuinely shocked the conqueror.

The Kalinga War stands alongside the Battle of Changping in Qin China as one of the two great massacres of the 3rd century BCE — both involved roughly comparable casualties, but only one ruler expressed regret.

Ashoka's response to Kalinga was unprecedented in the ancient world: he publicly declared that the suffering he had caused was a source of profound sorrow and vowed to govern by dharma rather than conquest.

title: The Rock Edicts
subtitle: the first ruler in history to carve his regrets into stone — dhamma as imperial policy
bullets: Ashoka's edicts were carved in Brahmi script on polished stone pillars and natural rock faces at sites across the empire, written in local Prakrit languages — the first substantial body of Indian writing after the Indus script.

The Fourteen Major Rock Edicts cover non-violence, religious tolerance, welfare of prisoners, public works, animal protection, and the appointment of Dhamma Mahamatras (morality officers) to oversee ethical compliance.

Rock Edict XII declares that all religious sects deserve respect and that criticizing others' beliefs only harms one's own — explicit religious tolerance as imperial policy, with no known precedent in the ancient world.

The edicts promote dhamma as non-sectarian moral principles transcending any single religion — Ashoka was not trying to convert the empire to Buddhism but to build a moral consensus all traditions could share.

Scholars debate whether the edicts reflect sincere conversion, political calculation, or both — most modern scholars lean toward both, noting that the texts are too personal for pure propaganda but too strategically deployed for private confession.

The nearest Western equivalent, Augustus's Res Gestae inscribed across the Roman Empire two centuries later, lists victories and honors with zero regret — Ashoka's edicts stand alone as the only ancient royal inscriptions expressing remorse for conquest.
Ashoka's experiment required its experimenter — the empire held together for twenty-six years after his conversion but fell apart within a generation of his death.

title: The Buddhist Emperor
subtitle: the Third Council, missionaries to Sri Lanka, and the launch of a world religion
bullets: Without Ashoka, Buddhism might have remained a regional Indian movement — his patronage, missionary infrastructure, and diplomatic connections launched it toward becoming a world religion.
The Third Buddhist Council at Pataliputra around 250 BCE, backed by Ashoka's imperial authority, codified Theravada orthodoxy and organized nine missionary expeditions to foreign lands.
Ashoka's son Mahinda led a Buddhist mission to Sri Lanka around 247 BCE that converted King Devanampiya Tissa and established a Buddhist tradition that has continued unbroken for over 2,200 years.
His daughter Sanghamitta brought a cutting from the Bodhi tree at Bodh Gaya to Sri Lanka — a descendant still grows at Anuradhapura, possibly the oldest documented tree in the world.

Buddhist missions reportedly reached the Hellenistic kingdoms of Syria, Egypt, Macedonia, Cyrene, and Epirus — Rock Edict XIII names these kings by name, proving Ashoka knew the Mediterranean political map.

The Gandhara school of Buddhist art, emerging in the centuries after Ashoka, produced the first visual depictions of the Buddha in recognizably Greek sculptural style — draped robes, naturalistic features.

The Barabar Caves near Bodh Gaya, carved on Ashoka's orders for the Ajivika sect, are the oldest surviving rock-cut caves in India — demonstrating his policy of supporting all religious communities, not just Buddhists.

title: The Pillar of Ashoka
subtitle: lion capitals, the Ashoka Chakra, and how a 3rd-century emperor became a modern nation's symbol
bullets: Ashoka's polished stone pillars, quarried from Chunar sandstone and transported hundreds of miles, display a distinctive mirror-like polish that modern engineers have been unable to fully replicate.

The Lion Capital from Sarnath — four lions standing back-to-back atop an inverted lotus, with an elephant, horse, bull, and lion on the abacus below — was adopted as independent India's national emblem in 1950.

The Ashoka Chakra, a 24-spoked wheel representing dhamma, appears on the Indian national flag — placed there by Nehru's government as a deliberate link between modern Indian democracy and Ashoka's ancient moral governance.

James Prinsep's decipherment of the Brahmi script in 1837 unlocked the Ashokan inscriptions for the first time in over two thousand years — before Prinsep, nobody could read the pillars that stood across the Indian landscape.

The pillars themselves are engineering marvels — monolithic columns up to 50 feet tall and 50 tons in weight, quarried, polished, transported, and erected without modern machinery across an empire spanning thousands of miles.

Nehru explicitly cited Ashoka as a model for the new Indian republic, arguing that Ashoka's combination of moral authority and political power offered a specifically Indian alternative to Western models of governance.

title: The Fall
subtitle: the Arthashastra vs. the Rock Edicts — two visions of power, neither sufficient alone
bullets: After Ashoka's death around 232 BCE, the empire fragmented within a generation — weak successors, provincial governors asserting independence, and Greco-Bactrian invasions from the northwest.

The last Maurya emperor Brihadratha was assassinated by his own general Pushyamitra Shunga during a military review in 185 BCE — the ease of the coup reveals how completely Maurya authority had evaporated.

The Arthashastra's empire had the machinery but not the legitimacy; Ashoka's empire had the legitimacy but not the machinery to survive the loss of its author — neither vision alone could hold the subcontinent together.
The Uttarapatha highway became the spine of every subsequent Indian road network — when the Mughals built the Grand Trunk Road two thousand years later, they followed the same route.

Every Buddhist temple in Thailand, every Zen garden in Japan, every statue of the Buddha in any museum anywhere is in a sense part of Ashoka's legacy — his missionary infrastructure launched Buddhism as a world religion.

The Brahmi script's descendants — Devanagari, Tamil, Bengali, Burmese, Thai, Tibetan — are used by roughly half the world's literate population, a legacy comparable to the Phoenician alphabet's spread.
A billion people carry Ashoka's lions and wheel on their money and flag — a daily reminder that one of the most powerful men in ancient history spent the second half of his life trying to be good.
